perturbation.original_text: Supplier shall maintain adequate books and records and retention samples consistent with cGMP Requirements and any other Applicable Law and requirements of applicable governmental or regulatory authorities, in respect of test records, samples and associated support data for all batches of Product Manufactured by Supplier sufficient to substantiate and verify Supplier's duties and obligations under this Agreement for [* * *] from the expiration date of the respective Product batch.
perturbation.changed_text: Supplier shall endeavor to maintain books and records and retention samples largely consistent with cGMP Requirements and other Applicable Law and requirements of governmental or regulatory authorities, in respect of test records, samples, and associated support data for all batches of Product Manufactured by Supplier to substantiate Supplier's duties and obligations under this Agreement for a reasonable time from the expiration date of the respective Product batch.
perturbation.explanation: The original text specifies a clear obligation to maintain adequate records. The modified text introduces ambiguity by using 'endeavor to maintain,' 'largely consistent,' and 'a reasonable time,' making the record-keeping obligation vague and difficult to enforce. This contradicts the specific requirements for record retention in pharmaceutical manufacturing.
perturbation.law_explanation: 21 CFR § 211.180 mandates specific requirements for record-keeping in pharmaceutical manufacturing, including accuracy, integrity, and availability of records. The modified text weakens these requirements by introducing vague terms like 'endeavor,' 'largely consistent,' and 'reasonable time,' allowing for subjective interpretation and potentially non-compliant record-keeping practices. This directly undermines the FDA's requirement for complete and accurate records to ensure product safety and quality.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act (FD&C Act) | FDA Skip to main content Skip to FDA Search Skip to in this section menu Skip to footer links In this section : Laws Enforced by FDA Home Regulatory Information Laws Enforced by FDA Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act (FD&C Act) Laws Enforced by FDA FD&C Act Reference Information United States Code, Title 21 The laws of the United States are organized by subject into the United States Code. The United States Code contains only the currently enacted statutory language. The official United States Code is maintained by the Office of the Law Revision Counsel in the United States House of Representatives. The Office of the Law Revision Counsel reviews enacted laws and determines where the statutory language should be codified related to its topic. The Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act and subsequent amending statutes are codified into Title 21 Chapter 9 of the United States Code. The listing of FD&C Act sections presented here identifies both the FD&C Act and U.S. Code section numbers, which can be used to narrow your search on the Law Revision Counsel website. To search the FD&C Act on the Law Revision Counsel website, use the Advanced Search to limit the your search to Title 21. To search the FD&C Act on the Law Revision Counsel website, you may either search by U.S. code section number or browse the Title 21 section listing . Content current as of: 03/29/2018 Back to Top
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity and weakens the original obligation to maintain adequate records, creating a contradiction. The law explanation specifies that 21 CFR § 211.180 mandates specific requirements for record-keeping in pharmaceutical manufacturing, including accuracy, integrity, and availability of records. The modified text uses terms like 'endeavor,' 'largely consistent,' and 'reasonable time,' which introduces subjectivity and could lead to non-compliant record-keeping practices. The scraped snippet 1, references Title 21, implying the modified text deviates from the standard requiring accuracy, undermining compliance.

perturbation.original_text: Supplier shall be responsible for Manufacturing the Product in compliance with Applicable Law, cGMP Requirements and the standards of any other applicable governmental or regulatory authority.
perturbation.changed_text: Supplier shall strive to Manufacture the Product in general accordance with Applicable Law, cGMP Requirements and the broader standards of applicable governmental or regulatory authority.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall be responsible for Manufacturing the Product in compliance with' to 'shall strive to Manufacture the Product in general accordance with' weakens the obligation, making compliance a goal rather than a requirement. The addition of 'broader standards' is vague and difficult to define. This is a violation of the law.
perturbation.law_explanation: 21 CFR § 210.1 requires strict adherence to current good manufacturing practices (CGMP). By using 'strive' and 'general accordance,' the modified text introduces a level of discretion that contradicts the mandatory nature of CGMP compliance required by the FDA. Pharmaceutical manufacturers must fully comply with CGMP, not just aim to do so.

perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the original obligation by changing 'shall be responsible for Manufacturing the Product in compliance with' to 'shall strive to Manufacture the Product in general accordance with.' The Law Explanation states that 21 CFR § 210.1 requires strict adherence to current good manufacturing practices (CGMP). The original text ensured compliance, while the modified text only aims for general accordance, which contradicts the mandatory nature of CGMP compliance. Scraped Law Snippet 1 references 21 CFR 210.1, which supports the need for strict adherence. The change from 'compliance' to 'strive' introduces ambiguity, undermining the legally required standard.

perturbation.original_text: In the event of any Recall or Seizure with respect to the Product during the Term of this agreement, Supplier shall, upon the written request of Customer, as soon as reasonably possible, supply replacement Product to Customer in an amount sufficient to replace the amount of Product Recalled or Seized, at the applicable then current Fees for Product under this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event of any Recall or Seizure with respect to the Product during the Term of this agreement, Supplier may, at their discretion, and upon the written request of Customer, attempt to supply replacement Product to Customer in an amount roughly equivalent to the amount of Product Recalled or Seized, at the then current Fees for Product under this Agreement if feasible.
perturbation.explanation: The original text places a responsibility on the supplier to provide replacement product in the event of a recall or seizure. The modified text uses the phrasing 'Supplier may, at their discretion, and attempt to supply' which introduces ambiguity by making the replacement of recalled or seized product discretionary rather than obligatory, and uses 'roughly equivalent' instead of 'sufficient to replace'.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 21 U.S. Code § 350b - Notification concerning recall and replacement of infant formula
perturbation.law_explanation: 21 U.S. Code § 350b(a) requires that companies notify the FDA when they discover that an infant formula they have introduced is adulterated or misbranded, and further clarifies that a company’s recall plan must address how it will deal with the situation. By making this discretionary, and not a guarantee, this is a direct contradiction to this section of law.

New dietary ingredients (a) In general A dietary supplement which contains a new dietary ingredient shall be deemed adulterated under section 342(f) of this title unless it meets one of the following requirements: (1) The dietary supplement contains only dietary ingredients which have been present in the food supply as an article used for food in a form in which the food has not been chemically altered. perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates the supplier to provide replacement product in the event of a recall or seizure. The changed text introduces ambiguity and discretion, using phrases like 'may, at their discretion, and attempt to supply' and 'roughly equivalent.' This weakens the supplier's obligation. The Law Explanation states that 21 U.S. Code § 350b(a) requires companies to have a recall plan that addresses how they will deal with a recall situation. By making the replacement of recalled products discretionary and not guaranteed, the modified text contradicts the intention of this law, as it undermines the responsibility for a company to address recalls adequately.
